aim: Design and prioritize curative genetic strategies for sickle cell disease that either correct the causal HBB p.Glu6Val (rs334, GAG to GTG, HbS) lesion or reactivate fetal hemoglobin, then rank candidate edits by their predicted effect on HbS polymerization, on-target and off-target editing precision in patient derived CD34+ hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells (HSPCs), and functional antisickling benefit after erythroid differentiation. The workflow spans structure and sequence modeling of the mutation and candidate edits (including adenine base editing to the naturally benign G-Makassar variant HbG, beta E6A), a systematic map of the fetal to adult hemoglobin switch centered on the BCL11A erythroid enhancer and the HBG1 and HBG2 gamma globin promoters, guide RNA and base editing window design and scoring, off target prediction, an editing plan in patient HSPCs with single cell and bulk erythroid readouts, and a functional antisickling validation plan.
hypothesis: The pathogenic hydrophobic contact created by valine at HBB position 6 (the acceptor pocket between the E and F helices of an adjacent deoxy hemoglobin tetramer, resolved in the deoxy HbS fiber) can be disrupted or bypassed by three genome editing strategies whose relative benefit is predictable in silico and testable ex vivo: (1) adenine base editing of the sickle codon from GTG to GCG installs the G-Makassar substitution (beta E6A), a clinically silent variant that does not polymerize; (2) disruption of the GATA1 motif in the BCL11A +58 erythroid enhancer (the exa-cel or Casgevy target) or (3) recreation of hereditary persistence of fetal hemoglobin (HPFH) point variants in the HBG1 and HBG2 promoters (for example -113 A>G, -175 T>C, -198 T>C) each derepress gamma globin and raise antisickling fetal hemoglobin (HbF) per cell above the roughly 20 to 30 percent pancellular threshold that abrogates polymerization. We predict that a base edit installing G-Makassar or a promoter edit achieving pancellular HbF induction yields the highest ratio of antisickling benefit to off target and bystander liability, and that candidate edits can be ranked before any wet lab work by combining structural, evolutionary sequence, and cis regulatory evidence.

steps.title: Anchor the causal lesion: HBB p.Glu6Val, the HbS fiber contact, and the clinical variant landscape
steps.what: Fix the exact molecular target before designing any edit. Retrieve the HBB gene model, the reference and sickle beta globin protein sequences, and the clinical annotation of the sickle allele, then locate the mutant residue on the polymer contact that drives disease. Confirm the causal variant is HBB rs334 (NM_000518.5 c.20A>T, legacy p.Glu6Val, mature chain numbering; note HGVS p.Glu7Val includes the initiator methionine) and pull its ClinVar clinical significance and population frequency. From UniProt P68871, map residue Glu6 (Val in HbS) and the E helix, F helix, and heme pocket. On the deoxy HbS crystal structure, identify the double strand lateral contact in which the mutant beta6 valine inserts into the acceptor pocket between the E and F helices of a neighboring tetramer, and catalog nearby residues (for example beta85 Phe and beta88 Leu of the acceptor) that define the polymerization interface every therapeutic edit must ultimately relieve. Build a residue level target dossier that later steps score against.
tools: Ensembl (HBB gene ENSG00000244734, GRCh38 chr11:5,225,464-5,227,071 reverse strand, transcript and exon 1 coordinates for the sickle codon)
ClinVar (VCV000015333, rs334, HbS pathogenic classification and gnomAD frequency)
UniProt (P68871 hemoglobin subunit beta, residue and secondary structure features, disease variant HbS)
PDB and molecular structure viewer (2HBS deoxy HbS double strand fiber; reference deoxy HbA for comparison)
Reactome (R-HSA-1480926 O2/CO2 exchange in erythrocytes, hemoglobin oxygen transport context)
outputs: Target dossier table: HBB genomic coordinates of the sickle codon, cDNA and protein HGVS strings, ClinVar significance, and population allele frequency
Annotated deoxy HbS fiber figure highlighting beta6 Val in the donor position and the E to F helix acceptor pocket with labeled contact residues
A residue and coordinate reference sheet used by all downstream editing and scoring steps

steps.title: Model HbS versus candidate corrective alleles in structure and by evolutionary sequence likelihood
steps.what: Quantify, in silico, how each amino acid change at beta6 (and each candidate edit) alters the polymerization contact and overall hemoglobin stability, so wet lab effort is spent on the most promising alleles. For the causal Val6 and for candidate substitutions (Ala6 for G-Makassar, the wild type Glu6, and any bystander products of the editing window such as beta7 changes), model the beta globin tetramer, dock or superpose onto the deoxy HbS double strand, and score loss of the hydrophobic donor contact and any change in tetramer stability. Use a structure predictor to generate mutant models where no crystal structure exists and to estimate stability effects, and use a genomic language model to score the sequence level tolerability and evolutionary plausibility of each candidate coding edit and of nearby noncoding regulatory edits (a proxy for whether an edit lands on a constrained versus tolerant position). Rank candidate alleles by combined structural disruption of the polymer contact and predicted variant tolerability, giving G-Makassar (Ala6) an explicit prediction of abolished polymer contact with retained oxygen transport.
tools: AlphaFold and protein structure prediction (mutant beta globin tetramer models for Val6, Ala6, Glu6 and bystander variants; per residue confidence and delta stability estimates)
PDB superposition and interface scoring (place each modeled tetramer onto the 2HBS donor and acceptor lattice, score the beta6 contact)

Evo 2 genomic language model (variant effect and sequence likelihood scoring for each candidate HBB coding edit and for candidate BCL11A enhancer and HBG promoter edits from later steps, to flag constrained positions and likely benign changes)
UniProt and ClinVar variant cross reference (annotate whether any bystander product corresponds to a known benign or pathogenic HBB variant)
outputs: Ranked allele table: for each candidate edit, predicted change in the polymer donor contact, predicted tetramer stability change, Evo 2 variant likelihood score, and any matching known human variant
Structural overlay figure comparing Val6 (disease), Ala6 (G-Makassar, predicted nonpolymerizing), and Glu6 (wild type) at the fiber contact
A shortlist of coding edits carried into guide and base editing design, with G-Makassar prioritized and rationale recorded

steps.title: Map the fetal to adult hemoglobin switch: the BCL11A erythroid enhancer and the HBG1 and HBG2 promoters
steps.what: Build the regulatory map for the HbF reactivation strategies so that enhancer and promoter edits are placed at validated functional bases rather than arbitrary sites. Assemble the beta globin locus regulatory architecture: the HBG1 and HBG2 (gamma globin) promoters, the master repressor BCL11A and its erythroid specific +58 intronic enhancer containing the GATA1 half site that exa-cel disrupts, and the gamma globin promoter repressor motifs (BCL11A binding around the -115 region and ZBTB7A near -200). Compile the catalog of naturally occurring HPFH point variants in the HBG promoters that raise HbF and protect from sickle cell disease (for example -113 A>G, -175 T>C, -195 C>G, -198 T>C), which define exactly the bases an editor should recreate. Use pathway and expression resources to relate BCL11A dosage to gamma globin derepression and to define erythroid lineage context. Cross reference public erythroid regulatory data (chromatin accessibility and transcription factor occupancy across erythroid differentiation) to confirm the enhancer and promoter windows are active in the relevant cell state.
tools: Ensembl (HBB locus and HBG1, HBG2, BCL11A gene models and coordinates; the BCL11A +58 erythroid enhancer interval and the GATA1 motif; regulatory build annotations)
Reactome (erythropoiesis and hemoglobin switching pathway context linking BCL11A to gamma globin repression)
ClinVar and curated HPFH variant catalogs (HBG1 and HBG2 promoter HPFH point variants with reported HbF elevation)

GEO (search for public CD34+ erythroid differentiation ATAC-seq, ChIP-seq for GATA1 and BCL11A, and RNA-seq series to confirm enhancer and promoter activity in erythroid cells; use the accessions reported in the study data availability statements rather than assuming a fixed accession)
UniProt (BCL11A and GATA1 protein and DNA binding domain annotation)
outputs: Regulatory map of the beta globin locus: BCL11A +58 enhancer GATA1 site coordinates, HBG1 and HBG2 promoter repressor motif coordinates, and the target bases for each HbF strategy
HPFH variant table listing each recreatable promoter variant, its coordinate, reported HbF effect, and the repressor motif it disrupts
Erythroid activity evidence panel (accessibility and factor occupancy tracks over the enhancer and promoters) confirming the windows are engaged in differentiating CD34+ cells

steps.title: Design and score the editing reagents: base editing to G-Makassar, enhancer disruption, and HPFH promoter edits
steps.what: Turn the prioritized targets into concrete, scored reagents for three parallel strategies. Strategy A, direct correction to G-Makassar: design an adenine base editing scheme that converts the sickle codon GTG to GCG (installing beta E6A) using an ABE with a compatible PAM (for example an NRCH PAM enabled ABE8e variant reported for this site), define the editing window, place the target adenine, and enumerate all bystander adenines with their predicted amino acid consequences. Strategy B, BCL11A enhancer disruption: design guide RNAs (Cas9 nuclease and, alternatively, base editors) targeting the GATA1 motif in the BCL11A +58 erythroid enhancer, the exa-cel class target. Strategy C, HPFH recreation: design base editors to install the HBG1 and HBG2 promoter HPFH variants (-113 A>G with an adenine base editor; -175 T>C and -198 T>C with cytosine or adenine base editors as the strand dictates) to disrupt repressor binding. Score every guide for on target activity, protospacer uniqueness, PAM availability, editing window position of the intended base, and bystander burden, and feed each candidate edit back through Evo 2 to flag any edit that lands on an evolutionarily constrained base.
tools: Base editor and guide RNA design software (adenine and cytosine base editor window modeling, on target efficiency prediction, PAM enumeration including SpCas9 NGG and engineered NRCH PAMs, bystander edit enumeration)
Cas9 nuclease guide design tools (for the BCL11A enhancer disruption arm)
Ensembl and reference genome retrieval (exact protospacer and PAM context sequences at each target)
Evo 2 genomic language model (re-score each designed edit and its bystander products for sequence tolerability and functional constraint)
UniProt and ClinVar (translate every coding bystander to its protein consequence and check against known benign or pathogenic HBB variants)
outputs: Reagent design table for all three strategies: guide or base editor identity, protospacer and PAM, target base position in the editing window, predicted on target efficiency, and full bystander list with amino acid or regulatory consequence
A prioritized reagent shortlist per strategy with an explicit rationale (for example the ABE scheme giving cleanest GTG to GCG conversion with minimal non silent bystanders)
Per edit Evo 2 constraint flags integrated into the ranking

steps.title: Predict genome wide off targets and safety liabilities for the top reagents
steps.what: Before proposing any HSPC editing, exhaustively predict off target and collateral risks for the shortlisted reagents so that only high specificity candidates advance. For each guide and base editor, run in silico genome wide off target nomination (sequence similarity search allowing mismatches and bulges, PAM aware) against the human reference, and for base editors additionally nominate off target windows where an off target protospacer would place a susceptible base in the activity window. Screen nominated sites for overlap with cancer associated genes, essential genes, and erythroid regulatory elements, and specifically check that BCL11A enhancer editing does not stray into BCL11A coding exons or its neurologically important non erythroid regulatory regions. Flag guanine rich or repetitive protospacers prone to promiscuous editing. Assemble a validation ready list of the top predicted off target sites (to be confirmed later by an unbiased empirical assay) and rank the reagents by a combined specificity and predicted safety score.
tools: In silico off target nomination tools (CRISPR mismatch and bulge tolerant genome search, PAM aware; base editor off target window scoring)
Ensembl and genome annotation (annotate each nominated off target by gene, element type, and functional category; enforce that BCL11A coding and non erythroid regulatory regions are avoided)
ClinVar and cancer gene resources (flag nominated off targets in clinically important or oncogenic loci)
Evo 2 (score whether a nominated off target edit would fall on a functionally constrained position, prioritizing sites for empirical follow up)
outputs: Ranked off target report per reagent: nominated sites with mismatch and bulge counts, genomic annotation, and predicted risk category
A specificity and safety score used to select the final lead reagent for each of the three strategies
A curated panel of top predicted off target sites earmarked for unbiased empirical confirmation in the wet lab plan

steps.title: Plan editing in patient derived HSPCs with erythroid differentiation and single cell readouts
steps.what: Specify a rigorous ex vivo editing experiment in patient derived cells with the molecular readouts that establish both editing and biological correction. Plan electroporation of the lead reagents as ribonucleoprotein (base editor mRNA or protein plus synthetic guide, or Cas9 RNP for the enhancer arm) into CD34+ HSPCs mobilized from persons with sickle cell disease (homozygous HbSS) and appropriate sickle trait and healthy donor controls, then in vitro erythroid differentiation over the standard multiphase culture to enucleated erythroblasts. Define genotype readouts (amplicon deep sequencing to quantify on target editing rate, bystander spectrum, indel background, and allelic conversion of GTG to GCG or promoter and enhancer edit fractions) and transcriptomic readouts. Use single cell RNA-seq across the erythroid differentiation trajectory to confirm on target editing does not distort erythroid maturation, to measure per cell HBG (gamma globin) versus HBB (beta globin) switching, and to detect any off lineage or stress signature; use bulk RNA-seq of sorted erythroblasts for HBG to HBB ratio and a global expression safety scan. Include an engraftment plan (xenotransplant of edited HSPCs to confirm editing persists in long term repopulating stem cells) as the stem cell durability readout, mirroring the standard for approved and investigational SCD editing programs.
tools: scRNA-seq analysis compute (erythroid trajectory and pseudotime, per cell HBG versus HBB quantification, maturation and stress signatures, detection of off lineage differentiation)
Bulk RNA-seq analysis compute (differential expression, HBG to HBB ratio, global safety scan comparing edited versus mock)
CRISPR and base editing amplicon analysis compute (on target editing rate, bystander and indel spectra, allele conversion quantification from targeted deep sequencing)
GEO (deposit and, for benchmarking, reuse public CD34+ SCD editing and erythroid differentiation scRNA-seq and bulk RNA-seq series identified from study data availability statements)
dbGaP (governed access framework for patient derived sequencing data; plan controlled access data use consistent with dbGaP style consent and data use limitations for patient HSPC sequencing)
outputs: Editing outcome table per reagent and donor: on target rate, bystander spectrum, indel background, and allele conversion in HSPCs and in differentiated erythroblasts
Single cell erythroid trajectory with per cell gamma to beta globin switching and maturation and safety signatures for edited versus control cells
Bulk RNA-seq HBG to HBB ratio and global safety comparison, plus an engraftment and long term repopulation plan for stem cell durability

steps.title: Functional antisickling validation plan: hemoglobin composition, polymerization, and red cell physiology
steps.what: Define the functional endpoints that prove edited cells resist sickling, connecting genotype to the disease phenotype. Specify hemoglobin fraction quantification in differentiated erythroblasts and reticulocytes (HPLC or capillary and mass based methods, and single cell HbF flow cytometry) to measure the corrected or induced species: percent HbG-Makassar and residual HbS for the correction arm, and pancellular percent HbF and F cell frequency for the enhancer and promoter arms, benchmarking against the roughly 20 to 30 percent per cell HbF threshold associated with abrogation of polymerization. Specify a direct polymerization and sickling assay panel: in vitro deoxygenation induced sickling imaging and quantification of the sickled cell fraction, hemoglobin polymerization or delay time measurement under controlled hypoxia, and red cell physiology (deformability by ektacytometry, density and dehydration, and oxygen saturation dependent behavior), with the explicit caveat that monoallelic correction yields a mixed HbG plus HbS cell that can remain partly dehydrated, so biallelic or high fraction editing is the target. Cross validate against the structural prediction from earlier steps (nonpolymerizing G-Makassar; HbF exclusion from the polymer) and against an empirical unbiased off target assay confirming the sites nominated in silico. Tie the whole workflow together by ranking the three strategies on achieved antisickling benefit versus editing precision and safety.
tools: Hemoglobin fractionation and quantification (HPLC or capillary electrophoresis and intact mass for HbG-Makassar, HbS, HbA, and HbF; single cell HbF and F cell flow cytometry)
In vitro sickling and polymerization assays (controlled deoxygenation sickling imaging, polymerization delay time under hypoxia)
Red cell physiology assays (oxygen gradient ektacytometry for deformability, density and dehydration measurement)

Unbiased empirical off target confirmation compute (analysis of a genome wide double strand break or base edit detection assay to confirm the top in silico nominated off targets from the specificity step)
AlphaFold and PDB cross check (reconcile the observed nonpolymerization of G-Makassar and HbF with the structural fiber contact model from earlier steps)
outputs: Antisickling endpoint table per strategy and donor: hemoglobin composition, single cell HbF or F cell frequency, sickled cell fraction under hypoxia, polymerization delay time, and deformability
Genotype to phenotype correlation linking editing rate and allele conversion to reduction in sickling, with the monoallelic HbG plus HbS caveat quantified

Final ranked recommendation across the G-Makassar correction, BCL11A enhancer disruption, and HBG promoter HPFH strategies, integrating antisickling benefit, editing precision, empirically confirmed off target burden, and stem cell durability

datasets.name: HBB gene, transcript, and the beta globin locus (BCL11A, HBG1, HBG2) coordinates and regulatory build
HbS sickle allele clinical annotation (rs334, NM_000518.5 c.20A>T, p.Glu6Val) with pathogenicity and population frequency
Hemoglobin subunit beta protein sequence, residues, secondary structure, and HbS disease variant
Deoxy sickle hemoglobin S double strand fiber structure (beta6 Glu to Val donor and acceptor lattice)
Erythrocyte oxygen transport and hemoglobin switching pathway context

CD34+ HSPC erythroid differentiation and BCL11A or HBG editing transcriptomic and epigenomic series (accessions taken from each study's data availability statement; search rather than assume a fixed GSE)
Governed access framework for patient derived HSPC sequencing (consent and data use limitations for controlled access)
Base editing HbS to HbG-Makassar improves hemoglobin function (ABE8e-NRCH GTG to GCG, beta E6A), primary reference for the correction arm
Adenine base editing of HBG1 and HBG2 promoters to recreate HPFH and induce fetal hemoglobin (BEACON study), primary reference for the promoter arm
Exagamglogene autotemcel (exa-cel, Casgevy) CRISPR disruption of the BCL11A erythroid enhancer, primary reference for the enhancer arm
datasets.source: PubMed (New England Journal of Medicine 2021, Frangoul et al., PMID 33283989)

datasets.name: BCL11A erythroid enhancer as the target for HbF reactivation (functional enhancer definition)
Saturating mutagenesis of the BCL11A enhancer identifies the GATA1 motif required for HbF repression

validation: The workflow is validated by concordance across independent evidence layers and against established clinical benchmarks, never by any single assay. Structural prediction (loss of the beta6 donor contact for G-Makassar; exclusion of fetal hemoglobin from the deoxy HbS polymer) must agree with the measured nonpolymerization and reduced sickling; Evo 2 sequence constraint calls must agree with the observed tolerability of the installed variant and of bystander products checked against ClinVar and UniProt. Editing claims require targeted amplicon deep sequencing (on target rate, allele conversion of GTG to GCG or promoter and enhancer edit fraction, bystander and indel spectra) and the in silico off target nominations must be confirmed by an unbiased empirical genome wide assay, with any confirmed off target in an essential, oncogenic, or erythroid regulatory locus disqualifying the reagent. Biological correction requires that per cell HbF for the reactivation arms exceed the roughly 20 to 30 percent pancellular threshold associated with abrogation of polymerization, that HbG-Makassar for the correction arm replace HbS with retained oxygen transport, and that hypoxia induced sickling, polymerization delay time, and red cell deformability improve relative to mock edited patient cells, with the monoallelic HbG plus HbS mixed cell (which can remain partly dehydrated) explicitly quantified rather than assumed corrected. Single cell RNA-seq must show preserved erythroid maturation without off lineage or stress signatures, and edited HSPCs must retain editing after xenotransplant to demonstrate durability in long term repopulating stem cells. All prioritized alleles, guides, and edits recreate or correct real human variants (G-Makassar, HPFH promoter variants, the exa-cel enhancer target); nothing is a fabricated or simulated allele, and each candidate is benchmarked against the corresponding published clinical or preclinical result rather than treated as novel in isolation.
figures: Deoxy HbS fiber contact map: beta6 valine in the donor position inserting into the E to F helix acceptor pocket, with the corrective substitutions Ala6 (G-Makassar) and Glu6 (wild type) overlaid and predicted to abolish the contact

Ranked candidate edit panel: for each edit, predicted structural disruption of the polymer contact, predicted stability change, Evo 2 variant likelihood, and matching known human variant, sorted by benefit to liability

Beta globin locus regulatory map: the BCL11A +58 erythroid enhancer GATA1 site and the HBG1 and HBG2 promoter repressor motifs, annotated with the exact target bases for enhancer disruption and for each recreatable HPFH variant

Reagent and off target dashboard: base editing windows for GTG to GCG and for the HPFH promoter edits with bystander adenines, guide protospacers and PAMs, and a ranked genome wide off target table annotated by gene and risk category

Ex vivo editing outcome and erythroid trajectory: on target editing rate and allele conversion in patient HSPCs and erythroblasts, alongside a single cell erythroid pseudotime colored by per cell HBG versus HBB switching for edited versus control cells

Antisickling validation summary: hemoglobin composition (percent HbG-Makassar or pancellular HbF versus residual HbS), sickled cell fraction and polymerization delay time under hypoxia, and red cell deformability, with a final strategy ranking integrating benefit, editing precision, confirmed off target burden, and stem cell durability
